name: config
context: tsconfig.json reference — compiler options, module resolution, paths, extends, include/exclude, and project setup
info: tsconfig.json is the configuration file for the TypeScript compiler. It controls every aspect of compilation: which files to include, how to resolve modules, what JavaScript target to emit, how strict type checking should be, and how the output is structured. Every TypeScript project should have a tsconfig.json in its root. The file supports inheritance via the `extends` field for sharing base configurations, glob-based file inclusion with `include`/`exclude`/`files`, path mapping with `paths` and `baseUrl` for module aliases, and project references for monorepo architectures. Understanding tsconfig.json is essential for agents working with TypeScript — incorrect configuration causes confusing type errors, broken imports, and build failures. The options are organized into categories: type checking, modules, emit, JavaScript support, interop, and completeness.
body:
# tsconfig.json Reference

tsconfig.json configures the TypeScript compiler. Place it in your project root. Run `npx tsc --init` to generate one with defaults.

## File Structure

```jsonc
{
  // Inherit from a base config
  "extends": "@tsconfig/node20/tsconfig.json",

  // Compiler options
  "compilerOptions": {
    // ... all options go here
  },

  // Which files to compile
  "include": ["src/**/*.ts"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist", "**/*.test.ts"],
  "files": ["src/global.d.ts"],

  // Project references (monorepos)
  "references": [
    { "path": "./packages/core" },
    { "path": "./packages/cli" }
  ]
}
```

## Type Checking Options

These control how strictly TypeScript validates your code.

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    // Master strict switch — enables ALL strict flags below
    "strict": true,

    // Individual strict flags (all enabled by strict: true)
    "noImplicitAny": true,           // Error on expressions/declarations with implied 'any'
    "strictNullChecks": true,         // null and undefined are distinct types
    "strictFunctionTypes": true,      // Strict checking of function parameter types
    "strictBindCallApply": true,      // Strict bind, call, apply methods
    "strictPropertyInitialization": true, // Class properties must be initialized
    "noImplicitThis": true,           // Error on 'this' with implied 'any' type
    "useUnknownInCatchVariables": true, // catch(e) is 'unknown' not 'any'
    "alwaysStrict": true,             // Emit 'use strict' in every file

    // Additional strictness (NOT included in strict: true)
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, // Index signatures return T | undefined
    "noImplicitOverride": true,       // Require 'override' keyword for overrides
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true, // Error on fallthrough switch cases
    "noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature": true, // Require bracket notation for index
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true, // Differentiate 'missing' from 'undefined'
    "noImplicitReturns": true,        // Error when not all paths return
    "noUnusedLocals": true,           // Error on unused local variables
    "noUnusedParameters": true,       // Error on unused function parameters
    "allowUnreachableCode": false,    // Error on unreachable code
    "allowUnusedLabels": false        // Error on unused labels
  }
}
```

**Recommendation**: Always set `strict: true`. Add `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true` and `noImplicitOverride: true` for maximum safety.

## Module Options

These control how TypeScript resolves and emits modules.

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    // Output module format
    "module": "nodenext",       // Node.js ESM + CJS dual
    // Other values: "commonjs", "es2015", "es2020", "es2022", "esnext", "node16"

    // How imports are resolved
    "moduleResolution": "nodenext",  // Modern Node.js resolution
    // Other values: "node" (legacy), "node16", "bundler" (for webpack/vite)

    // Module output target
    "target": "es2022",         // JavaScript version to emit
    // Common values: "es2020", "es2021", "es2022", "es2023", "esnext"

    // Available runtime APIs
    "lib": ["es2023"],          // Type definitions for runtime APIs
    // DOM projects: ["es2023", "dom", "dom.iterable"]

    // Module interop
    "esModuleInterop": true,    // Allow default imports from CJS modules
    "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true,  // Allow default import syntax
    "verbatimModuleSyntax": true, // Enforce import/export type annotations (5.0+)
    "isolatedModules": true,    // Ensure files can be transpiled independently

    // Resolve JSON imports
    "resolveJsonModule": true   // Allow importing .json files
  }
}
```

### Module Resolution Guide

| Project Type | `module` | `moduleResolution` |
|-------------|----------|--------------------|
| Node.js library | `nodenext` | `nodenext` |
| Node.js app (ESM) | `nodenext` | `nodenext` |
| React/Vite/webpack | `esnext` | `bundler` |
| Legacy Node.js | `commonjs` | `node` |
| Deno | `esnext` | `bundler` |

## Path Mapping

Create import aliases to avoid deep relative paths.

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["src/*"],
      "@utils/*": ["src/utils/*"],
      "@types": ["src/types/index.ts"],
      "~/*": ["src/*"]
    }
  }
}
```

This lets you write `import { helper } from "@/utils/helper"` instead of `import { helper } from "../../../utils/helper"`. Note: `paths` only affect TypeScript resolution — your bundler or runtime must also be configured to resolve these aliases (e.g., Vite's `resolve.alias`, webpack's `resolve.alias`, or `tsconfig-paths` for Node.js).

## Emit Options

Control what files tsc produces.

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "./dist",              // Output directory
    "rootDir": "./src",              // Root of source files (preserves structure)
    "declaration": true,             // Generate .d.ts files
    "declarationDir": "./dist/types", // Separate directory for declarations
    "declarationMap": true,          // Source maps for declarations
    "sourceMap": true,               // Generate .js.map files
    "inlineSourceMap": false,        // Inline source maps in JS (alternative)
    "inlineSources": false,          // Include TS source in source maps
    "removeComments": true,          // Strip comments from output
    "noEmit": false,                 // Set true for type-check only
    "noEmitOnError": true,           // Don't emit if there are errors
    "emitDeclarationOnly": false,    // Only emit .d.ts (bundler handles JS)
    "importHelpers": true,           // Use tslib for helper functions
    "downlevelIteration": true,      // Emit helpers for for..of on older targets
    "newLine": "lf",                 // Line ending: "lf" or "crlf"
    "preserveConstEnums": true,      // Keep const enum declarations in output
    "incremental": true,             // Cache for faster rebuilds
    "tsBuildInfoFile": "./.tsbuildinfo" // Incremental cache location
  }
}
```

## Extends — Configuration Inheritance

Share base configurations across projects.

```jsonc
// tsconfig.base.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true
  }
}

// tsconfig.json
{
  "extends": "./tsconfig.base.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "target": "es2022"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}
```

### Community Base Configs

The `@tsconfig` npm scope provides recommended bases:

```bash
npm install -D @tsconfig/node20
npm install -D @tsconfig/strictest
npm install -D @tsconfig/recommended
```

```jsonc
{
  "extends": "@tsconfig/node20/tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "dist"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}
```

## Include, Exclude, and Files

```jsonc
{
  // Glob patterns for files to compile
  "include": [
    "src/**/*.ts",
    "src/**/*.tsx"
  ],

  // Glob patterns to exclude (defaults include node_modules)
  "exclude": [
    "node_modules",
    "dist",
    "**/*.test.ts",
    "**/*.spec.ts"
  ],

  // Explicit file list (overrides include/exclude)
  "files": [
    "src/global.d.ts",
    "src/env.d.ts"
  ]
}
```

Rules:
- If `files` is specified, those files are always included
- `include` defaults to `["**/*"]` (everything)
- `exclude` defaults to `["node_modules", "bower_components", "jspm_packages"]` plus `outDir`
- Files referenced by `import` are always included regardless of `exclude`
- Use `npx tsc --explainFiles` to debug which files are included and why

## Recommended Configurations

### Node.js Backend

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es2022",
    "module": "nodenext",
    "moduleResolution": "nodenext",
    "lib": ["es2023"],
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "declaration": true,
    "declarationMap": true,
    "sourceMap": true,
    "incremental": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}
```

### React/Vite Frontend

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es2020",
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "lib": ["es2023", "dom", "dom.iterable"],
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "allowImportingTsExtensions": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "verbatimModuleSyntax": true
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

### Library (Dual CJS + ESM)

```jsonc
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es2020",
    "module": "nodenext",
    "moduleResolution": "nodenext",
    "lib": ["es2023"],
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src",
    "strict": true,
    "declaration": true,
    "declarationMap": true,
    "sourceMap": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist", "**/*.test.ts"]
}
```

sections.title: Strict mode options
sections.body: The `strict: true` flag enables all strict-family options at once: `noImplicitAny`, `strictNullChecks`, `strictFunctionTypes`, `strictBindCallApply`, `strictPropertyInitialization`, `noImplicitThis`, `useUnknownInCatchVariables`, and `alwaysStrict`. Always start with `strict: true` for new projects. You can disable individual flags if needed. Additional strict options NOT covered by the `strict` flag: `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` (index signatures return `T | undefined`), `noImplicitOverride` (require `override` keyword), `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` (distinguish missing from `undefined`), `noFallthroughCasesInSwitch`, `noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature`.

sections.title: Module resolution strategies
sections.body: The `moduleResolution` option controls how TypeScript finds imported modules. `nodenext`/`node16`: follows Node.js ESM resolution rules — requires file extensions in imports, respects `exports` field in package.json, distinguishes ESM and CJS. `bundler`: follows bundler conventions — no file extensions needed, resolves `exports` field, supports `allowImportingTsExtensions`. `node` (legacy): the classic Node.js resolution algorithm — no `exports` field support, resolves index files automatically. Use `nodenext` for Node.js libraries and `bundler` for frontend projects using webpack, Vite, or esbuild.

sections.title: Path aliases and baseUrl
sections.body: The `paths` compiler option maps import specifiers to file locations: `"@/*": ["src/*"]` lets you write `import { x } from "@/utils"`. Requires `baseUrl` to be set (usually `.`). `paths` only affects TypeScript's type resolution — your bundler or runtime must separately understand these aliases. For Vite, configure `resolve.alias`. For webpack, configure `resolve.alias`. For Node.js at runtime, use `tsconfig-paths` or `tsx` which reads paths automatically. With `moduleResolution: "nodenext"`, `paths` may not work as expected — prefer `imports` in package.json instead (Node.js subpath imports).

sections.title: Configuration inheritance with extends
sections.body: The `extends` field inherits compiler options from a base tsconfig. The extending config's options override the base. `include`, `exclude`, and `files` are NOT inherited — they must be redeclared in each config. You can extend from: a relative path (`./tsconfig.base.json`), an npm package (`@tsconfig/node20/tsconfig.json`), or a multi-level chain. Multi-file tsconfig patterns: `tsconfig.json` (default, references others), `tsconfig.build.json` (production build), `tsconfig.test.json` (includes test files). Use `npx tsc --showConfig` to see the final merged configuration.

sections.title: Recommended configurations by project type
sections.body: **Node.js backend**: `target: "es2022"`, `module: "nodenext"`, `moduleResolution: "nodenext"`, `lib: ["es2023"]`. **React/Vite frontend**: `target: "es2020"`, `module: "esnext"`, `moduleResolution: "bundler"`, `lib: ["es2023", "dom", "dom.iterable"]`, `jsx: "react-jsx"`, `noEmit: true`. **Library**: `target: "es2020"`, `module: "nodenext"`, `declaration: true`, `declarationMap: true`. All should use `strict: true`, `skipLibCheck: true`, `esModuleInterop: true`, `forceConsistentCasingInFileNames: true`, and `isolatedModules: true`.
